evidence.reasoning: Both PDB metadata and the paper identify Chain E as 'Nanobody 35' and describe its role as stabilizing the receptor-G protein complex.
evidence.quote_or_path: polymer_components[2].description; The cells also co-expressed G\[beta] and G\[gamma], and the purifi-cation was performed in the presence of recombinant Nb35 nanobodies 58 to help stabilize the receptor-G protein complex. (page 3, 'Purification...' section)

key_findings: The structure reveals the human sweet receptor's (TAS1R2) transmembrane (TM) domain in an inactive pre-coupled state when bound to a G-protein complex.
Identification of specific TAS1R2 residues (Y756 and F827) in the TM domain and C-terminal tail that are crucial for G-protein alpha subunit contact and receptor signaling.
Confirmation of the G-protein coupling occurring via the TAS1R2 subunit, not TAS1R3.

evidence.quote_or_path: cryo-EM structure of the human sweet receptor bound to two of the most widely used artificial sweeteners-sucralose and aspartame. Our results reveal the structural basis for sweet detec-tion, provide insights into how a single receptor mediates all our responses to such a wide range of sweet-tasting compounds, and open up unique possibilities for designing a generation of taste modulators informed by the structure of the human receptor. (page 2, Summary); In order to obtain the structure of the human sweet receptor in the agonist-bound state, we generated receptor complexes in the presence of the artificial sweeteners sucralose or aspartame; (page 3, 'Purification...' section)
evidence.reasoning: The paper explicitly states that sucralose is an artificial sweetener that binds to the receptor to initiate a signaling cascade, and the structure was generated in the agonist-bound state in the presence of sucralose.

g_protein.note: The G alpha s subunit (GNAS2_HUMAN) is a miniGs/gust chimera (with the C-terminus from GNAT3) and is further fused to the C-terminus of the TAS1R3 receptor subunit.

evidence.reasoning: The paper explicitly states that the TM domains in this specific particle class (Class 3, which PDB 9O38 represents) adopt an 'inactive pre-coupled state' and compares it to inactive mGluR2.
evidence.quote_or_path: the orientation of the TMs in TAS1R2, together with analysis of the low-resolution TAS1R3 density, suggests an inactive pre-coupled state, as pre-viously reported for the mGluR2 receptor (Figures S8E and S8F; see legend for details). (page 7); Note that the sweet receptor in the class 3 particle set adopts a conformation with a TM3-TM3 interface, which is comparable to the conformation of the inactive mGluR2 (TM3-TM4) ... but different from the TM arrangement in the active state of family C receptors (TM6-TM6...). (Figure S8 caption)

structure_info.note: Transmembrane (TM) domains only from Class 3 particles.
